title: 10 Way-Too-Early Oscar Predictions and the Best Movies at Telluride and Venice
format: Two festival dispatches and a bet on the season. Amanda works her first European fest at the 80th Venice (Campari, boats, reserved seats) and brings back a ranked five. Sean does four days of altitude and tents at Telluride and brings back his own. Then ten way-too-early Oscar predictions and a best-picture top ten that's mostly still unreleased.

segments.heading: Amanda's Venice Five
segments.note: the 80th Venice International Film Festival, favorite last
segments.subject: Venice

films.title: Priscilla
films.note: No. 1. Sofia Coppola's Elvis-marriage film with Cailee Spaeny and Jacob Elordi. Amanda extended her trip for the red-carpet premiere and admits she's fully in the bag: vintage Coppola in the Marie Antoinette / Virgin Suicides vein, done well.

films.title: The Killer
films.note: No. 2. David Fincher's hitman movie, adapted by Andrew Kevin Walker from the graphic novel. Amanda found it very funny and kept thinking of Sean, a guy in a half-zip with his tunes, trying to get the job done.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: No. 3, held back so she and Sean could take it together. Yorgos Lanthimos and Emma Stone's fourth collaboration; both loved it.

films.title: Ferrari
films.note: No. 4 and the first film Amanda saw. Michael Mann with Adam Driver as Enzo Ferrari and Penélope Cruz; the racing scenes are the draw. Italian festivalgoers were unconvinced by Driver's accent.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: No. 5. Bradley Cooper's Leonard Bernstein film. Amanda was moved to tears in stretches, cooler on others, and suspects she liked different things in it than most of the raves did.

films.title: Evil Does Not Exist
films.note: Ryusuke Hamaguchi's follow-up to Drive My Car, a quiet chamber piece and a change of pace after the festival's louder competition titles. Recently passed over as Japan's international feature submission.

films.title: The Beast
films.note: Bertrand Bonello's three-timeline film with Léa Seydoux and George MacKay, one of the most acclaimed titles of the festival. Amanda found it Lynch-derivative and didn't connect, but wanted credit for sitting through the whole thing.

films.title: The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar
films.note: Wes Anderson's Roald Dahl short, out of competition. Anderson picked up the Cartier-sponsored Glory to the Filmmaker Award. Classic Wes, and, Amanda argues, correctly not stretched to a feature.

segments.heading: Sean's Telluride Five
segments.note: the 50th Telluride Film Festival, favorite last
segments.subject: Telluride

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: No. 1 and the best thing Sean saw. Jonathan Glazer's first film in a decade, an Auschwitz-adjacent domestic snapshot loosely off the Martin Amis novel. Harrowing and hard to talk about, possibly the most avant-garde movie ever to land a Best Picture nod.

films.title: The Taste of Things
films.note: No. 2. Tran Anh Hung's 19th-century French food romance with Juliette Binoche, one of the more sensual movies made about cooking, filed beside Babette's Feast and Big Night. The emotional inverse of Zone of Interest.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: No. 3 and the movie of the festival season, discussed jointly. A hard, big-laughs comedy that lands every swing, with a scene-stealing Mark Ruffalo. Emma Stone already the odds-on Best Actress favorite.

films.title: The Bikeriders
films.note: No. 4 and the first film Sean saw. Jeff Nichols's motorcycle-club rise-and-fall with Tom Hardy, Austin Butler, and Jodie Comer's aggressive Chicago accent. Indebted to Scorsese: Goodfellas on motorcycles, reductively speaking.

films.title: All of Us Strangers
films.note: No. 5. Andrew Haigh's grief drama with Andrew Scott, Paul Mescal, Claire Foy, and Jamie Bell. The big weepy of the year, though it didn't quite get Sean there. He singles out Jamie Bell.

segments.heading: The rest of Sean's Telluride
segments.note: honorable mentions and the ones he's mixed on

films.title: The Royal Hotel
films.note: Kitty Green's outback thriller with Julia Garner and Jessica Henwick as two travelers working a shit-kicker bar in the middle of nowhere. Sean's biggest surprise: a tense, conventionally entertaining movie with a couple of really good ideas.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: Alexander Payne back to basics, reuniting with Paul Giamatti for the first time since Sideways as a teacher stuck at a boarding school over the holidays. Softer than Payne's best because he didn't write it; newcomer Dominic Sessa is a discovery.

films.title: Saltburn
films.note: Emerald Fennell's Oxford class thriller with Barry Keoghan, Jacob Elordi, and a great Rosamund Pike. Gorgeous and wildly indebted to The Talented Mr. Ripley, but, like Promising Young Woman, some script choices left Sean confounded.

films.title: Rustin
films.note: George C. Wolfe's biopic of civil-rights organizer Bayard Rustin, with Colman Domingo the reason to watch. Conventional, right-down-the-middle Academy stuff, on Netflix soon.

films.title: Nyad
films.note: Free Solo directors Jimmy Chin and Elizabeth Chai Vasarhelyi's narrative debut, with Annette Bening as the long-distance swimmer and Jodie Foster as her coach. Sean liked learning the sport more than he liked the choices.

films.title: The Pigeon Tunnel
films.note: Errol Morris's John le Carré documentary, built on le Carré's last-ever interview. Sean says the double-agent theme comes through effectively.

films.title: La Chimera
films.note: Alice Rohrwacher's film, which Sean liked, good enough that he calls out Josh O'Connor. It screened alongside Rohrwacher's Silver Medallion, one of three handed to directors this year because of the strikes.

segments.heading: The Way-Too-Early Best Picture Top 10
segments.note: Sean's ranking as of September 6, seven of the ten still unreleased
segments.subject: Best Picture

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: No. 1, and still the Best Picture favorite despite a weekend of festival raves.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: No. 2. Played Cannes, goes wide October 20 with no preview screenings; Apple turned down Telluride's request for it.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: No. 3. Feels like a nine-or-ten-nomination movie, with Emma Stone out front for Best Actress.

films.title: Barbie
films.note: No. 4. The predicted haul narrows to screenplay, Best Actress, and a near-certain Original Song.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: No. 5 and the shakiest of the top five: a deeply personal Bradley Cooper movie that Netflix can't campaign while the strikes hold.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: No. 6. Played very well to the older Telluride crowd; Sean has notes, but it's the Academy.

films.title: All of Us Strangers
films.note: No. 7, and the vehicle for Sean's Andrew Scott Best Actor prediction.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: No. 8, one of Sean's international picks he treats as a firmer lock than the domestic titles above it.

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: No. 9. Already released and campaigning, which is exactly why it feels safer than the unseen movies.

films.title: Anatomy of a Fall
films.note: No. 10. Neon's Cannes winner, out in October; Sandra Hüller already on the cover of The Hollywood Reporter.

segments.heading: The acting races
segments.note: predictions built around movies still to come

films.title: Freud's Last Session
films.note: Anthony Hopkins for Sony Pictures Classics, a Best Actor wild card you never count out.

films.title: American Fiction
films.note: Cord Jefferson's debut; Jeffrey Wright reportedly excellent, still sight-unseen for both hosts.

films.title: May December
films.note: Todd Haynes's new one, with Charles Melton hotly tipped in the supporting race.

films.title: The Iron Claw
films.note: Named among the contenders still to arrive, alongside a stack of unreleased studio bets.
